title: “I Didn’t Know Where to Turn”—How One Family Found Help After a House Fire
bodyHeavy:
When a kitchen fire displaced the Nguyen family from their Brooklyn Center apartment in January, they had no renters insurance and no relatives nearby. With two school-age children and a newborn, Linh Nguyen spent the first night at a hotel paid for by the Red Cross, unsure what would happen next.

A caseworker at Hennepin County connected the family with Community Emergency Assistance Programs, better known as CEAP, which has operated out of Brooklyn Center for more than forty years. Within 48 hours, staff had secured a furnished two-bedroom unit through a landlord partnership, delivered a crib and kitchen basics, and enrolled the family in emergency SNAP benefits. A benefits navigator sat with Linh through every application — medical assistance, child care assistance, utility emergency aid — and followed up by phone each week.

The children returned to school within a week. The infant's pediatric appointments were rescheduled to a clinic within walking distance of the new unit. Linh, who had been working part-time as a dental assistant, was referred to a workforce counselor to help her add hours without jeopardizing her benefits eligibility. Six weeks after the fire, the family signed a longer-term lease on the same unit.

"I kept waiting for somebody to hand me a list of phone numbers," Linh said. "Instead they made the calls with me."
bodyMedium: A kitchen fire left the Nguyen family homeless in Brooklyn Center. Within 48 hours, CEAP helped them find temporary housing, furnishings, and a benefits navigator. Their story shows how community organizations step in when disaster strikes.
bodyLight: How one Brooklyn Center family found help after a house fire displaced them.

meta.deck: After a kitchen fire left them homeless, the Nguyen family found a community that showed up—not with a list of numbers, but with real help.

title: Thousands Expected at Region’s Largest Winter Food Drive
bodyHeavy:
Over 40 organizations will converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center this Saturday for the annual Fill the Shelves winter food drive. Organizers expect more than 3,000 attendees and hope to collect 50,000 pounds of non-perishable food items — roughly twice last year's haul, which staff attributed to a milder December that softened demand at the pantries they serve.

This year's event comes as food shelves across the metro report record intake. Second Harvest Heartland, the region's largest food bank, has warned partner agencies that winter reserves are running thin. Volunteers from across the metro have signed up to sort, pack, and distribute donations to food shelves in all five core metro counties, with priority routing to pantries in Brooklyn Center, Richfield, and the East Side of St. Paul that have seen the steepest spikes in first-time visitors.

Most-needed items, according to the coordinating committee, are shelf-stable protein, low-sodium canned vegetables, cooking oil, and culturally specific staples such as masa harina, basmati rice, and halal canned meat. Organizers will also accept unopened hygiene supplies, which are not covered by SNAP.

Free parking is available in the Convention Center ramp, and Metro Transit is offering free rides on Routes 5, 14, and 22 for anyone showing a Fill the Shelves flyer. Doors open at 9 a.m. and the drive runs until 4 p.m.
bodyMedium: Over 40 organizations converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center this Saturday for the annual Fill the Shelves drive, expecting 3,000+ attendees and 50,000 pounds of food donations.
bodyLight: Annual Fill the Shelves food drive this Saturday at the Convention Center.

title: How a Block Club in Phillips Organized Its Own Warming Station
bodyHeavy:
When the city ran out of warming center capacity during a bitter cold snap in February, neighbors on 25th and Bloomington Avenue opened their church basement. Temperatures that week bottomed out at 22 below, and the nearest municipal warming site had been closed for the night when a resident first called the block club's group text at 10 p.m.

Within three days they had space heaters, donated blankets, a rotating schedule of volunteers, and enough hot coffee to keep fifty people warm overnight. Donations of socks, hand warmers, and instant oatmeal came from households within four square blocks; a neighbor who owns a cleaning company brought an industrial-size urn of coffee each evening. The church, which shares the corner with an auto body shop and a Somali mall, contributed fuel for the heaters and left the side door unlocked.

The block club coordinated with Minneapolis Emergency Management and Hennepin County outreach workers to ensure guests could connect with shelter referrals by morning. Two county staffers began rotating through the basement to do intake and schedule follow-up appointments — a handoff that organizers say kept the space from becoming a de facto shelter with no exit.

It started as an act of desperation, organizers said, but could become a model for neighborhood-level emergency response. The block club is now drafting a memorandum of understanding with the county so the protocol can activate faster next winter.
bodyMedium: When the city ran out of warming center capacity, neighbors on 25th and Bloomington opened their church basement and kept fifty people warm overnight. It started as desperation and became a model.
bodyLight: Phillips neighbors opened a church basement warming station during the cold snap.

title: Hennepin County Pilots Guaranteed Income Program for 200 Families
bodyHeavy:
Hennepin County announced a two-year guaranteed income pilot that will provide $500 per month to 200 families earning below 30% of area median income. Participants were selected by lottery from roughly 4,800 applicants in North Minneapolis and Brooklyn Park zip codes with the highest rates of housing instability, eviction filings, and emergency shelter use.

County officials say the program is funded by federal ARPA dollars and will be evaluated by the University of Minnesota Humphrey School, which will track outcomes including housing stability, employment, child well-being, and use of county safety-net programs. Researchers will compare outcomes to a control group of applicants who were not selected in the lottery but agreed to participate in periodic surveys.

Payments will be delivered via prepaid debit card and are structured so they do not count as income for most federal benefits. A benefits counselor will be available throughout the pilot to help families navigate interactions with SNAP, medical assistance, and housing subsidies — an issue that tripped up similar pilots in St. Paul and Minneapolis when participants lost partial benefits after receiving payments.

Payments begin in April. Earlier Hennepin County guaranteed-income experiments have focused on specific populations such as formerly homeless adults; the new pilot is the county's largest to date and its first to include families with children as the primary target group.
bodyMedium: Hennepin County will give $500/month to 200 low-income families in a two-year guaranteed income pilot. Payments begin in April, funded by federal ARPA dollars.
bodyLight: Hennepin County launches $500/month guaranteed income pilot for 200 families.

title: Inside the Push to Build 1,000 Affordable Units Along the Green Line
bodyHeavy:
A coalition of nonprofit developers and community organizations is pressing the Met Council to fund 1,000 new affordable housing units along the Green Line corridor between downtown Minneapolis and downtown St. Paul. The proposal targets vacant and underused parcels within a quarter-mile of stations, including surface parking lots owned by public agencies and several decommissioned commercial buildings along University Avenue.

Advocates say the corridor’s transit access makes it ideal for car-free households, many of whom are immigrants and refugees who already rely on the Green Line for work and school. Aeon, Project for Pride in Living, and CommonBond Communities have each committed pre-development funding, contingent on Met Council participation. The coalition has identified twenty-two candidate sites and estimates construction could begin on the first phase within eighteen months of funding approval.

Opponents on some city councils worry about density and parking, particularly in Midway neighborhoods where residents have raised concerns about the pace of development. Planning staff in St. Paul have signaled that zoning changes adopted after the Green Line opened in 2014 already permit the heights proposed, but that parking minimums could require variances at several sites.

The Met Council is expected to vote in May. If the full 1,000 units are funded, it would be the largest transit-oriented affordable housing commitment in the region since the Blue Line extension was first planned.
bodyMedium: A nonprofit coalition is pushing the Met Council to fund 1,000 affordable units along the Green Line. Advocates cite transit access for car-free households; a vote is expected in May.
bodyLight: Coalition pushes for 1,000 affordable units along the Green Line corridor.

meta.deck: Nonprofit coalition targets vacant parcels along the Green Line for car-free affordable housing.

title: Winter Shelter Beds Available Tonight—Walk-ins Welcome
bodyHeavy:
Overflow shelters open at Simpson Housing (2740 1st Ave S) and Mary’s Place (401 N 7th St). No ID required and no appointment needed. Temperatures are forecast to drop to −8°F tonight with wind chills approaching −25°F, a level at which Hennepin County activates its cold-weather emergency protocols.

Beds are first come, first served. Doors open at 7 p.m. and close at 7 a.m. Guests receive a cot, blanket, access to restrooms, and a light meal. Pets are welcome at Simpson Housing; Mary’s Place does not accommodate pets but will coordinate with Animal Humane Society for short-term fostering on request. Both sites have staff on hand who speak Spanish, Somali, and Oromo, and translation by phone is available in other languages.

Metro Transit routes 5 and 14 stop within one block of both shelters and will run on regular schedule overnight. Riders who cannot pay fare should board and ask the operator; Metro Transit has suspended fare enforcement during the cold-weather emergency. Call 612-874-8683 for transport assistance if you cannot reach a shelter on your own; a van from St. Stephen's Human Services can pick up callers anywhere in Hennepin County between 5 p.m. and midnight.

If you know someone sleeping outside tonight, outreach teams are actively checking encampments and can be reached through the same number.
bodyMedium: Overflow shelters open tonight at Simpson Housing and Mary’s Place. No ID required. Temps dropping to −8°F. Call 612-874-8683 for transport help.
bodyLight: Shelter beds available tonight. No ID required. Call 612-874-8683.

title: Boil Water Advisory—North Minneapolis
bodyHeavy:
A water main break at 26th and Lyndale has prompted a precautionary boil water advisory for residents between 24th and 28th Streets, from Hennepin Avenue east to Nicollet Avenue. Affected households should boil tap water for at least one minute before drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, or preparing baby formula until further notice. Water used for washing hands, showering, and laundry does not need to be boiled.

The advisory will remain in place until two consecutive rounds of bacterial testing come back clear, a process that typically takes 24 to 48 hours after repairs are complete. City crews are on site and expect pipe repairs to take 24 to 48 hours, with the testing window beginning after water service is fully restored.

Free bottled water is available at Whittier Park recreation center from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m. while the advisory is active. Residents can take up to two cases per household per day; those without transportation can call 311 to request delivery from a volunteer through the Minneapolis Emergency Management neighbor-help line.

Hennepin County Public Health advises parents of infants and medically fragile residents to rely on bottled water throughout the advisory period. Updates, including the results of each testing round, will be posted at minneapolismn.gov/water and shared through the city's text alert system. Residents can sign up for alerts by texting WATER to 468311.
bodyMedium: Water main break at 26th & Lyndale. Boil water before drinking. Free bottled water at Whittier Park rec center, 8am–8pm. Repairs expected in 24–48 hours.
bodyLight: Boil water advisory: 24th–28th Streets, North Minneapolis.

title: Emergency Shelter & Housing Help
bodyHeavy:
Emergency shelters, coordinated entry points, and housing assistance programs in the Twin Cities. If you are experiencing homelessness, start by calling Hennepin County Coordinated Entry at 612-348-9410 or Ramsey County at 651-266-4100. Coordinated entry is the primary way to be matched with both emergency shelter and longer-term housing programs, and a single intake call puts you on the list at multiple providers.

Shelter beds fill quickly — call early in the day. Most single-adult shelters begin taking reservations between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m., and walk-in slots typically fill by mid-afternoon on cold nights. Families with children receive priority placement and can usually be accommodated same-day through People Serving People or Mary’s Place; unaccompanied youth under 24 should ask to be routed to YouthLink, which operates a dedicated drop-in center in downtown Minneapolis.

If you have nowhere safe to stay tonight and cannot reach coordinated entry, Hennepin County's after-hours line continues intake through the evening and can dispatch outreach workers to encampments, bus stations, and hospital waiting rooms. Survivors of domestic violence should call the statewide Day One hotline at 1-866-223-1111 for shelter routing that keeps addresses confidential.

Bring what identification you have, but none is required to receive emergency shelter. Caseworkers can help replace lost IDs, Social Security cards, and birth certificates through county-funded document programs once you are stabilized.
bodyMedium: Emergency shelters and housing help in the Twin Cities. Hennepin: 612-348-9410. Ramsey: 651-266-4100. Call early—beds fill quickly.

title: When the Plow Doesn't Come: A Winter in Rural Aitkin
bodyHeavy:
Last February's ice storm left dozens of seniors in McGregor and Palisade without cleared driveways for nearly a week. County road crews reached township roads first, but private driveways — many of them a quarter-mile long and banked by snow the plow had thrown back — were left to homeowners or whoever they could call. For residents on oxygen, dialysis, or home-delivered meals, that's not an inconvenience; it's an emergency.

A volunteer-run snow response network emerged from that storm, organized through Aitkin County's senior services office. Staff matched a list of known high-risk residents against a directory of neighbors who volunteered tractors, ATVs with plow attachments, and pickup trucks. The network now connects homeowners to plow-equipped neighbors within a three-hour radius, with a dispatcher working from the senior services office during active storms.

The program is strictly volunteer. No money changes hands, and the county does not verify equipment or insurance — an arrangement that would be unusual in a metro county but mirrors how rural townships have always handled mutual aid. Organizers say the trust is built over decades; the spreadsheet just formalizes it.

Since November the network has logged 240 clearing trips across Aitkin County, with heaviest demand in the Big Sandy Lake and Hill City areas. County staff say the model has drawn inquiries from senior services offices in Itasca, Crow Wing, and Kanabec counties considering similar programs ahead of next winter.
bodyMedium: After last February's ice storm stranded rural seniors for days, a volunteer snow response network now connects them to plow-equipped neighbors through Aitkin County senior services.
bodyLight: Aitkin's volunteer snow response network keeps rural seniors connected during storms.

meta.deck: How neighbors built what the county couldn't scale — a rural snow response network.

title: Coon Rapids Launches Warming Bus Pilot After Deadly Cold Snap
bodyHeavy:
After two people died of exposure in Coon Rapids and Blaine during January's cold snap, Anoka County Public Health launched a mobile warming bus pilot that circulates between the Coon Rapids Civic Center, the Blaine Walmart lot, and Riverdale Station from 8 p.m. to 6 a.m. on sub-zero nights. The bus is a retrofitted Metro Transit coach with seating, a small restroom, coffee, and an outreach worker on board who can make referrals to shelter, detox, or medical care.

Both deaths in January occurred within walking distance of open businesses, but neither person made it indoors. Public health officials say their review of the cases pointed to a gap the county had not previously acknowledged: Anoka's overnight shelter capacity is concentrated in a single site that requires sobriety and a brief intake, and there were no 24-hour drop-in spaces outside of hospital emergency rooms. The warming bus is intended to fill that gap without duplicating shelter infrastructure.

The route was chosen in consultation with Anoka County sheriff's deputies and outreach workers from Alexandra House and Lee Carlson Center, who flagged the three stops as locations where people had been found sleeping outdoors during previous cold snaps. Riders can stay on the bus for the full overnight loop or disembark at any stop.

The pilot runs through April and will be evaluated for permanent funding based on ridership, referrals made, and whether cold-related emergency calls decline in the served corridor.
bodyMedium: Anoka County's new warming bus pilot circulates overnight between Coon Rapids, Blaine, and Riverdale on sub-zero nights. Two deaths during January's cold snap catalyzed the program.
bodyLight: Mobile warming bus pilot launches after cold snap claimed two lives in Anoka County.

title: Detroit Lakes' Summer Workforce Squeeze: When Staff Can't Afford to Live Here
bodyHeavy:
Three Detroit Lakes restaurants closed early last summer not because of slow business — but because they couldn't find staff who could afford to live in Becker County. Owners cited line cooks who commuted 45 minutes from Pelican Rapids, servers sharing a one-bedroom with three other workers, and a revolving door of seasonal hires who quit when they couldn't land housing close to the job.

Short-term rentals have gutted the long-term housing market in the county's tourism economy. A 2025 assessment by the Headwaters Regional Development Commission found that the number of homes listed on short-term rental platforms within the Detroit Lakes city limits had tripled since 2019, while long-term rental listings in the same period dropped by roughly half. Local employers, the school district, and Essentia Health's regional hospital are all competing with vacation platforms for the same housing stock.

A new coalition of employers is piloting employer-supported housing. The group — which includes the hospital, the school district, two resorts, and a manufacturing firm — has jointly purchased a 12-unit apartment building near downtown and plans to sublet units to workers at below-market rents tied to length of employment. A second coalition is exploring a master-lease arrangement with a rural developer.

City officials are also considering a short-term rental cap or licensing fee, though similar proposals have stalled in other northern Minnesota tourism towns. A workforce housing study commissioned by the county is due this summer.
bodyMedium: Detroit Lakes' summer economy runs on workers who can't afford to live in the county. Short-term rentals have gutted long-term housing. A new employer-housing coalition is piloting a response.
bodyLight: Becker County's tourism economy is colliding with its housing shortage.

title: Bemidji's Cold-Weather Shelter Ran Out of Beds Every Night in February
bodyHeavy:
Bemidji's only adult overflow shelter hit capacity every single night in February, turning away dozens of people to sleep in cars, hospital waiting rooms, or the doorways of closed storefronts along Paul Bunyan Drive. Staff at the shelter, which is operated by a partnership between local churches and Beltrami County social services, said they counted 37 nights in a row on which every cot was full by 9 p.m.

Community organizers are pushing the city and county to open a second site in partnership with Red Lake Nation's social services — a collaboration that has existed informally for years but never at this scale. A significant share of shelter guests are enrolled members of Red Lake, Leech Lake, or White Earth, and advocates say a jointly staffed site could shorten the travel distance for people moving between reservation services and the city, while also bringing cultural and health resources that the existing shelter cannot provide.

The proposal under discussion would repurpose a vacant wing of a former assisted-living facility, adding roughly 40 beds and a daytime drop-in space. Funding would come from a mix of state cold-weather shelter grants, tribal health dollars, and Beltrami County levy funds. County commissioners have asked staff to return with a cost estimate and operating plan before a vote in May.

In the meantime, organizers have set up a volunteer driver network to take people turned away at the shelter door to hospital lobbies and all-night gas stations considered safer than the street.
bodyMedium: Bemidji's adult overflow shelter turned away people every night in February. A new cross-jurisdictional partnership with Red Lake Nation is being pushed to open a second site.
bodyLight: Bemidji's shelter hit capacity nightly in February. Organizers push for second site.
